question.text: Gopal writes his will on a plain paper without any stamp duty and does not register it with the sub-registrar. His son later questions the validity of the will. Under Section 18 of the Registration Act, which statement about will registration is CORRECT?

answer.text: Registration is compulsory for all wills; without it, the will is automatically void
A will must be written on stamp paper of appropriate value to be valid
Registration is compulsory and can only be done during the testator's lifetime
Registration is NOT compulsory; a will can be on plain paper without stamp duty and is still valid

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under Sec 18 of the Registration Act, registration of a will is NOT compulsory. A will can be written on plain paper with no stamp duty. However, registration provides advantages: it is tamper-proof and ensures safe custody. Registration can be done during lifetime or by the legatee after the testator's death. **The Trap:** Many candidates believe registration is mandatory. The advantages of registration (tamper-proof, safe custody) are often confused with it being compulsory. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM X-B tests will formalities extensively. Note the special rule: a non-Hindu bequeathing to charity must deposit the will with the registrar within 6 months AND the testator must survive 12 months after execution. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** While registration is optional, always recommend it to clients. An unregistered will on plain paper is legally valid but much easier to challenge in court.
